perturbation.original_text: The obligation to confidentiality shall apply for a period of two years beyond the end of the collaboration.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligation to confidentiality shall apply for a period of six months beyond the end of the collaboration.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text shortens the confidentiality period to six months, which may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Swiss law, especially considering the length and nature of the collaboration. This creates a potential legal contradiction if a longer protection period is warranted.
perturbation.law_explanation: Art. 162 of the Swiss Code of Obligations addresses trade secrets and their protection. While it doesn't explicitly define a minimum protection period, a six-month period may be deemed insufficient given the nature of trade secrets involved in a business collaboration, leading to potential unenforceability under Swiss law, which generally looks at what is reasonable and customary in the specific industry.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Fedlex DE: Vielen Dank, dass Sie sie die Website des Bundesrechts aufgerufen haben; sie ist nur mit einem Javascript-fähigen Browser verfügbar. Um diese Website optimal nutzen zu können, empfehlen wir Ihnen, einen aktuellen Browser zu verwenden. FR: Merci d'avoir rejoint le site du droit fédéral; celui-ci n'est disponible qu'avec un navigateur supportant javascript. Afin de profiter d'une expérience maximale sur ce site, nous vous conseillons d'utiliser un navigateur récent. IT: Grazie per aver scelto il sito web del diritto federale; questo è disponibile soltanto con un browser che supporta JavaScript. Per navigare in modo ottimale in questo sito, vi consigliamo di utilizzare un browser più recente. RM: Grazia fitg che Vus visitais la pagina d’internet dal dretg federal; ella è disponibla mo cun in navigatur che sustegna javascript. Per pudair far diever da questa pagina d’internet en moda optimala As recumandain nus d’utilisar in navigatur actual. EN: Thank you for visiting the federal law website; it is only fully accessible if you are using a JavaScript-capable browser. In order to be able to use all the features of this site, we recommend that you use an up-to-date browser. Um diese Website optimal nutzen zu können, empfehlen wir Ihnen, einen aktuellen Browser zu verwenden. In order to be able to use all the features of this site, we recommend that you use an up-to-date browser.
perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the confidentiality period from two years to six months, potentially contradicting Swiss law regarding trade secret protection (Art. 162 of the Swiss Code of Obligations). While Art. 162 isn't provided, the LLM Law Explanation states that a six-month period may be deemed insufficient, leading to potential unenforceability, which creates a legal contradiction. The Scraped Law Snippets do not provide contradictory evidence. Therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall bear any costs that it generates or incurs in relation to the conclusion and execution of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Business Partner shall bear all costs that are generated or incurred in relation to the conclusion and execution of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Shifting all costs to the Business Partner may be deemed unconscionable or against public policy under Swiss law, especially if there's a significant power imbalance between Bomatec and the Business Partner. This could render the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Art. 21 of the Swiss Code of Obligations addresses unconscionable contracts. Assigning all costs to one party, particularly if it results in significant disadvantage to that party and benefits the other disproportionately, could be viewed as unconscionable and therefore unenforceable under Swiss law.

EN: Thank you for visiting the federal law website; it is only fully accessible if you are using a JavaScript-capable browser. In order to be able to use all the features of this site, we recommend that you use an up-to-date browser.
perturbation.justification: The original contract stated that "Each party shall bear any costs that it generates or incurs". The changed contract assigns all costs to the Business Partner. The Law Explanation states that Art. 21 of the Swiss Code of Obligations addresses unconscionable contracts. The Perturbation explanation states that shifting all costs to the Business Partner may be deemed unconscionable under Swiss law. The changed text directly contradicts the principle of fairness and could be deemed unconscionable, thus contradicting Art. 21 of the Swiss Code of Obligations. While scraped snippet 2 references javascript, it is unrelated. The assignment of all costs to one party creates a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Approval only applies to the current assignment or project and should be sought from Bomatec by the Business Partner on every occasion that collaboration is repeated.
perturbation.changed_text: Approval only applies to the current assignment or project, and Bomatec is under no obligation to provide such approval for repeated collaborations.
perturbation.explanation: This modified text allows Bomatec to arbitrarily deny the Business Partner the ability to reference the collaboration in the future, potentially harming the Business Partner's reputation and business prospects. Swiss law requires good faith in contract execution, and this clause could violate that principle.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 2 of the Swiss Civil Code (ZGB) embodies the principle of good faith. Bomatec's unrestricted ability to deny approval for the Business Partner to use their name as a reference could be viewed as bad faith, especially if the Business Partner's business heavily relies on references and the denial is without reasonable justification. This can create an imbalance violating the good faith provision.

In order to be able to use all the features of this site, we recommend that you use an up-to-date browser.
perturbation.justification: The changed text gives Bomatec unrestricted power to deny approval for the Business Partner's use of the collaboration as a reference, which could violate the principle of good faith under Swiss law, as explained in the Law Explanation, if the denial is unreasonable and impacts the Business Partner's business, creating an imbalance. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by stating this could harm the Business Partner's reputation and business prospects, violating the good faith principle. While there are no helpful scraped snippets, the scenario still strongly suggests a legal contradiction.
